## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hey guys, my name is Dean. I run the software house in Germany where we build web apps and also AI optimizations for our clients called Nocta.dev. And I also run a paid ads agency called ADXP. You can find it on Dean.co. I've been running native ads now for the past six years. I ran one of the top three campaigns worldwide in terms of spend on native. And maybe important to mention, I still run to this day ads on native for everything. I talk about this essentially my own experience or the experience here we have here in the agency. I'm sure most of you know already about native ads and know it as a traffic source, but I'd like to cover a few basics before we start. I think that native is a very powerful traffic source because much like YouTube, users arrive with like a certain intent and time. And that essentially means that unlike to matter or TikTok, you're not trying to pull users out of their fast scrolling habit. Native users, they actively choose to engage with your content. They click because they want to read or consume your app tutorial. Also think about it. No one opens their favorite online magazine if they only have like 15 seconds before their cap arrives. But they will open Instagram 100%. And therefore, that's in my opinion, a big difference. So native really captures attention when people are mentally ready to consume. And that gives us like a huge room to sell something. If it's via a short jump page, like in an example of a VSL, which we talk about, start mid or long form avatars, we go into that in a second. But that makes like a big difference. And we have many cases where we even split tested traffic source, which has like a very high intent such as Google search, for example, where we split tested a top five page for a certain vertical on Google search with an advertorial funnel on native for the same vertical. And we noticed that the AOV is way higher on native. So the native audience is around like 55 plus, right? Home owners, it's a kind of like high quality traffic coming from IT in most cases, publishers like CNN, yahoo.com, MSN.com, et cetera.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

The classic native funnel and user experience is like I said before, people come from an ad to an advertorial and then go to your lead form or product page. And that's I'd say the case for like 99% of the offers, right? There are products which are super commoditized where people really just need that one tipping point. And then you don't need an advertorial. For example, OZMPIC nowadays, people really just need to be activated on it and then they will buy already. They are very aware and educated that OZMPIC, just in this example, is the solution for their problems or desires. But yeah, the classic user journey is through an advertorial. We go into that in a second. I think it's also very important to mention that native, even though those traffic sources claim it different, native has no real user data. So unlike meta or Google, it's not like profile, cookie or pixel based. Essentially it has no real data. If someone visits CNN.com, checks the lifestyle category, clicks on your ad there, it is more or less tagged from the lifestyle category. There may be a few more data sets they have, but it's essentially it. And that essentially means that you cannot do like your proper targeting on native. So your ads have to do the targeting. So always keep in mind that the native audience is in general kind of like older audience, I'd say like around 55 plus and therefore try to market an offer which works and resonates well with the native audience. It might sound weird, but there are like certain offers where you can already tell before and that this won't work. For example, if you would ask me if I would want to advertise an online course for a 30 day handstand program, I can tell you already that this won't work. While if you would want to advertise a VSL offer for diabetes, blood sugar, this would work very, very well. And I see that day in, day out. A great thing to mention about native is that native can use unlike other big traffic sources redirects. So while on Meta or Google or TikTok, when you use over the course of a few months too

## [06:03 - 09:06]

many redirects, most likely they will ban your account. For native, it's like the classic funnel that the marketers are using, so-called link trackers, Red Track Volume, KTarrow, Click Magic or else. And there you can perform a redirect. And this essentially enables you as a marketer to test many, many angles and different formats for an angle at the same time. And you have like real split tests. In my opinion, if you split test in a separate campaign on Meta, another landing page, you have no real split test. It is maybe a similar audience, but it's not the same exact audience. While on native, if you perform a split test through your link tracker, through the redirects, you send if one publisher, let's say CNN.com, sends like 100 clicks, 50 clicks will go to the one page and 50 clicks would go to the other page. So we use that often that we split test for a certain service or product, the angles on different landing pages, and then even port that angle to another traffic source because we know already it works quite well with the audience. We have some data on it. We see the landing page, the R-conversion rate, et cetera. So that's also a great system to use native if the offer works in general for the audience as a test, more or less, for your offer and then port the offer plus the angle to another traffic source. The also important to mention is that we have a limited inventory on native. It is obviously not as big as as Meta with Facebook, the audience network, and also Instagram, et cetera, or Google with YouTube, Dimanche, et cetera. And therefore, you have a limited inventory and that essentially means that it's quite difficult to get to a certain spend, obviously, anyway, depending on the geo, but let's say you would put your budget 200K a day. It's quite rare that you would spend that 100K, even in a big geo like the US, for example, but that all the stars would need to be aligned. So you would need to have a weight loss offer in January, super high CTRs and CPMs, which I will talk about in a second. And then you might be able to spend that amount for a couple of days, but I'm pretty sure this won't hold. And just keep in mind that that's like a limited inventory. And now I'm going to share why it's so important to have therefore high CPMs.

## [09:06 - 11:08]

Usually on other traffic sources, you want to have low CPMs, obviously, because those CPMs are more or less dictating your costs. Right. And on native, it's the opposite. You want to have high CPMs because following that logic, high CPMs mean you have a higher spending potential, but you don't want to have high CPMs based on high CPCs. You want to have high CPMs based on high CTR and low CPCs, so we make sure we are always on target. And that's more or less the golden formula. You want to have high CPMs with high CTRs and low CPCs. And we'll go through that in a second, and I'll show you how you can do that for almost every vertical where it works for the native audience, just like I said before. But it's just something you would always need to keep in mind when running on native ads. I think it's also quite interesting to understand that since native doesn't have so much user data or doesn't have so much insights on the audience, et cetera, that also means that you essentially, you will always understand what's going on in the performance-wise. I think everyone who runs Google, like YouTube ads or meta ads, they know how it is that you are getting on one day a super strong audience pocket. While on the following day, you are being punished with high CPMs because of a bad user comment, and therefore you have terrible CPAs. On native, it's different. On native, you will see all the time you can do the breakdown in your link tracker or in the traffic source itself, and you can understand where the traffic was coming from. Did it convert or not? Do I want to increase the bid on that publisher in particular or not?
